title: practical english usage - Part 116
content:
- I've just bought some books on computing. (A limited number.) not number.) Our  next-door neighbours are students. (The main idea is classification, - We got talking to some students. (A limited number.) I like roses. (No idea of number.) doesn’ t say how  many.) - We've planted some roses in the garden. (A limited number; the speaker quantities, or not thinking about numbers/quantities at all. Compare: many. We   prefer no article when we  are thinking about unlimited numbers or numbers or quantities - when we  don’ t know, care or say exactly how  much/ We   prefer some/any when we  are thinking about limited but rather indefinite 2 some/any          or  no   article?

questions and negatives (for details, ►     161).

Some is used especially in affirmative sentences; any is more common in We need (some) cheese. I didn't buy (any) eggs.

no article. There is not always a great difference of meaning.

Uncountable and plural nouns can often be used either with some/any or with 1  use   with    uncountable         and   plural     nouns no   article 139 the   difference        between       some     /any     and the;   my,    your,          this,    that, Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;        etc    Section  12 grammar •  140   talking in general For the use of the + adjective to generalise about groups (e.g. the old, the blind), ►     188.

Do you like horses? (     D  you like a horse?) individuals.) extinct. The  sentence is about the whole tiger family, not about not The tiger is in danger of becoming extinct, (     A tiger is in danger of become^ the members of a group together.

Note that we  cannot use a/an in this way when we  are generalising about all of A baby deer can stand as soon as it's born. A child needs plenty of love.

(meaning ‘ any’ ) with a singular countable noun.

We   can also generalise by talking about one example of a class, using a/an The violin is more difficult than the piano.

Life would be quieter without the telephone.

musical instruments.

This is common with the names of scientific instruments and inventions, and Schools should concentrate more on the child and less on exams.

countable noun.

Sometimes we  talk about things in general by using the with a singular 2 generalisations           with    singular      countable        nouns not Most of the children got very tired, (     The most of the children . ..) not Most birds can fly. (     Fhe-most birds-.. .) Note that most (meaning ‘ the majority of) is used without the.

Nothing can travel faster than light.

- Why has the light gone out?

Would you like to travel into space?

- Write your name in the space at the bottom of the page.

She's very interested in nature, especially animals and birds.

- I never really understood the nature of my father's work.

It's not always easy to fit in with society.

- I've joined the local Dramatic Society.

I love cheese.

- ‘ Where's the cheese? T ate it.' not Life is complicated, (     Thedife-...) - I'm studying the life of Beethoven. (= one particular life) not Books are expensive, (     The books are expensive.) - Move the books off that chair and sit down. (= particular books) mean 'all’ . Instead, we  use no article. Compare: general - to talk about all books, all people or all life, for example. The does not We   do not use the with uncountable or plural nouns to talk about things in 1  the    does    not   mean     'all' 140 talking     in  general               _________ 141 grammar •        the: difficult cases did at school or the poverty I grew up in). In these ‘ half-general’  expressions,  -» rather general ideas (compared with the music we heard last night, the history I Britain, we  are not talking about all music, history or poverty, but these are still particular.
